attributes.stem: A 67-year-old man has a routine endoscopy as part of his Barrett's oesophagus surveillance program. He does not have any gastrointestinal symptoms and the heartburn with which he initially presented is now well controlled with a proton pump inhibitor, esomeprazole. He is otherwise well and has no other significant comorbidities. On this occasion, the endoscopy confirmed a presence of 5 cm long segment of Barrett's oesophagus with no evidence of erosive oesophagitis. 4 quadrant biopsies at 2 cm intervals show no evidence of dysplasia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following management approaches is most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has long-segment Barrett's oesophagus (>3 cm) without dysplasia, which carries an approximate annual risk of progression to cancer of approximately 0.5% per year for long-segment Barrett's. Repeat endoscopy in 2-3 years is the most appropriate option in this setting. This interval balances the risk of progression to dysplasia or cancer against the burden and risks of frequent endoscopy.

Patients should be counselled on the risks and benefits of Barrett's oesophagus surveillance and the surveillance strategy should be regularly reviewed. Factors that might influence the decision to continue or discontinue surveillance include the patient's age, life expectancy, ability to tolerate endoscopy, and ability to undergo potential interventions if dysplasia or cancer were detected. No further follow-up could be considered if the burden of ongoing surveillance were too great or impractical, but given the patient's good health and tolerance of surveillance thus far, ongoing surveillance would be recommended.

Repeat endoscopy in 6 months would be indicated if there were dysplastic changes, which are not present here. This frequent surveillance would expose the patient to unnecessary procedural risks without clear benefit.

Repeat endoscopy in 4-5 years is too long an interval for long-segment Barrett's, though this might be appropriate for short-segment disease (<3 cm). The longer the segment, the higher the risk of progression to dysplasia or cancer.

Radiofrequency ablation is an endoscopic eradication therapy reserved for patients with dysplasia. It involves thermal ablation of the Barrett's mucosa, allowing regeneration with normal squamous epithelium. It would be overly aggressive in this case of non-dysplastic Barrett's.
learningPoints: Barrett's oesophagus is defined as metaplastic columnar mucosa in the tubular oesophagus, with histological evidence of intestinal metaplasia.
Rate of cancer progression is higher in patients with long segment Barrett's oesophagus (>= 3cm) and dysplastic features on biopsy.
The risks and benefits of Barrett's oesophagus surveillance should be discussed with each patient and considered in the context of their other health issues and priorities.
Surveillance intervals are stratified based on segment length and presence of dysplasia.
references: Shaheen NJ, Falk GW, Iyer PG, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Barrett's Esophagus: An Updated ACG Guideline. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117(4):559-587. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259184/

Whiteman DC, Appleyard M, Bahin FZ et al. Australian clinical practice guidelines for the diagnosis and management of Barrett’s esophagus and early esophageal adenocarcinoma. J Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2015; 30:804-20.

Weusten BLAM, Bisschops R, Dinis-Ribeiro M, et al. Diagnosis and management of Barrett esophagus: European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (ESGE) Guideline. Endoscopy. 2023;55(12):1124-1146. Available at: https://www.thieme-connect.com/products/ejournals/abstract/10.1055/a-2176-2440
